coredata.dc:title: Simple and green route for fabrication of a nanostructured of the graphene‐Fe3O4@PANI for the photovoltaic activity

Sadegh, Negar
Bahrpeyma, Abdolhamid
Shabzendedar, Sahar
Kerman, Kagan
Noroozifar, Meissam
coredata.dc:description: In this work, for the first time, Gr (graphene)|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposite was synthesized from graphite, aniline-HCl and FeCl3.6H2O via a simple in-situ and green route as the active layer for photovoltaic application. The proposed nanocomposite was characterized by FT-IR, SEM, TEM, UV-vis spectroscopy, XRD technique, and then the thermal, magnetic, electrochemical, and photovoltaic properties investigated. The energy gap was calculated, and it is noticed that the optical band gap of nanocomposite fallows an indirect transition, arising from a strong spin-orbit coupling created by Fe3O4 in the Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposite. Next, polymer solar cell (PSC) based on this nanocomposite was fabricated with a simple device structure of FTO│TiO2│Gr|Fe3O4@PANI│Al. The photovoltaic properties of the cells have been evaluated under AM 1.5G illumination at 60 mW/cm2 with a solar simulator. A power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 1.81% was observed with a short circuit current (Jsc) of 4.14 mA/cm2, an open-circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.71 V, which indicates this kind of conjugated polymer should be a promising material in the application of polymer solar cells.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, for the first time, Gr (graphene)|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposite was synthesized from graphite, aniline-HCl and FeCl3.6H2O via a simple in-situ and green route as the active layer for photovoltaic application. The proposed nanocomposite was characterized by FT-IR, SEM, TEM, UV-vis spectroscopy, XRD technique, and then the thermal, magnetic, electrochemical, and photovoltaic properties investigated. The energy gap was calculated, and it is noticed that the optical band gap of nanocomposite fallows an indirect transition, arising from a strong spin-orbit coupling created by Fe3O4 in the Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposite. Next, polymer solar cell (PSC) based on this nanocomposite was fabricated with a simple device structure of FTO│TiO2│Gr|Fe3O4@PANI│Al. The photovoltaic properties of the cells have been evaluated under AM 1.5G illumination at 60 mW/cm2 with a solar simulator. A power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 1.81% was observed with a short circuit current (Jsc) of 4.14 mA/cm2, an open-circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.71 V, which indicates this kind of conjugated polymer should be a promising material in the application of polymer solar cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Today, due to the advances that human beings have made in various industries, the need for energy and energy storage devices is felt more than ever [1]. Many non-renewable energy sources such as oil, gas, etc., which are used, are being destroyed and reduced [2,3]. According to studies, due to population growth and increasing human consumption of these resources, none of these resources will exist in less than 100 years. With the spread of science and the advances that human beings have made throughout their lives, it can generate energy from renewable sources such as the sun, wind, and water which are good alternatives to fossil fuels [4,5]. Natural sources of sunlight can be used by solar cells to be a new source of energy. One type of solar cell is the polymer solar cell [6]. Based on photovoltaic phenomena in its active layers, sunlight is absorbed and converted into electrical energy [6,7]. Therefore, according to research in the field of production of light-sensitive polymers to convert light into electricity, it was found that the amount of electrical energy efficiency in this type of material is significant [8]. Today, the fabrication of polymer-carbon nanocomposites has received much attention. Placing the polymeric material between the layers of the carbon compound (such as graphite) improves their mechanical, thermal, and electrical properties [9–11]. Polyaniline is one of the conductive polymers whose composite preparation with graphene to improve the photovoltaic properties has attracted much attention [12–14]. The use of graphene as a combination agent with a polymer causes the simultaneous coverage of two mechanisms that increase the photovoltaic property, which is: 1) high electron mobility on graphene improve electron transfer and separation, 2) the ultrafast response speed towards light and the tunable conductivity [15]. It is universally recognized that graphene has great potential in the construction of light and stable light-controlled devices and systems [16]. For example, Liu et al. [17] exhibited that rGO can significantly improve the optical and X-ray photon response in CsPbBr3/rGO nanocomposites as compared with pure CsPbBr3 nanoparticles. Dio et al. [18] reported a NO2 gas sensor based on MoS2/rGO/Cu2O. The sensitivity of a metal oxide semiconductor can be obviously enhanced by the incorporation of rGO. As the results showed highest response to 500 ppb of the NO2 at room temperature, which is 11 times higher than the pure MoS2.

In a polymer-graphene-based solar cell system, electrons excited in a semiconductor can be transferred from the conduction band to the graphene surface through a diffusion mechanism. A Schottky barrier is created at the interface, which is the area separating the load carriers. To adjust and synchronize Fermi energy limits, electrons tend to flow from a higher Fermi energy level to a lower Fermi energy level [19]. The two-dimensional structure and π–conjugation of graphene help conduct electrons and leads to the rapid transfer of charge carriers and the effective separation of electron-hole pairs [20,21]. In addition, the use of metal oxides loaded on the surface of graphene has attracted the attention of many researchers due to its magnetic, thermal, mechanical, optical, and other applications [22,23]. One of the most famous metal oxides is Fe3O4, which is used in various fields due to its unique electrical properties, non-toxic, and environmentally friendly [24,25].

For the first time, we have recently developed an in-situ synthesis of magnetic nanocomposites from polyaniline derivatives and Fe3O4 by a simple, one-step and green method, and their applications in new polymer solar cells [26,27]. Furthermore, we have recently demonstrated that Fe3O4 could increase triple-state and then increase solar cell yield of nanocomposites fabricated from PANI derivatives [26,27]. Now we wish to report a combination effect of graphene as well as Fe3O4 in solar cells. This study explored an in-situ and one-step solid-state strategy to construct Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites as an active layer with good performance in polymer solar cells, which may open a new perspective for the preparation of excellent nanocomposites by solid-state chemical method, simple, green, stable, and low-cost.

Aniline hydrochloride (Aniline.HCl), iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3.6H2O), and graphite powder were all purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and Merck companies and used as received. Nano TiO2 paste (PST-20T, composed of 20 nm TiO2 nanoparticles) and FTO (Fluorine doped tin oxide, SnO2/F coated glass slide) were purchased from Sharif Solar Co. (Tehran, Iran).

Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer of the nanocomposites was obtained by using a JASCO spectrum (Kyoto, Japan) using KBr pellets (frequency range 4000–400 cm−1). X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) were recorded on a Philips PW 1830 diffractometer (Netherlands) using CuKα radiation in a range of 10–80o. Magnetic measurements were performed using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, MicroSense EV9) at room temperature (RT) with a maximum magnetic field of 5000 H (Oe).

The microstructure of the nanocomposites was examined with a scanning electron microscope (SEM S-360) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM Philips/ CM-10 Netherlands). UV–Vis spectra were recorded in N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) and formic acid (FA) on a Shimadzu UV-1800 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The composition of the powders was checked with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) measurements done with an X-ray microanalysis unit (Cambridge Instruments) attached to SEM. Thermal properties of the nanocomposites were tested by thermo-gravimetric (TGA) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) analysis technique (DuPont Instruments, Fayetteville, NC). The heating rate was 10 °C min−1, and samples were scanned from 25 to 900°C in a nitrogen atmosphere. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) test was measured using an auto lab PGSTAT302 N electrochemical station (Metrohm AG, Utrecht, The Netherlands) at 25°C. All measurements were carried out in a standard three-electrode cell. A modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 3 mm in diameter) was used as a working electrode. A mixture containing 3.0 mg of nanocomposite and 1 mL of NMP was homogenized, and then drop-casting the GCE surface and dried in RT. A Pt wire and an Ag/AgCl electrodes were served as counter and reference electrodes in 0.10 M phosphate buffer electrolyte (pH 2.0), or acetonitrile (CH3CN) solution, including tetrabutylammonium perchlorate (Bu4NClO4), 0.10 M, was used as the supporting electrolyte. The electrolyte was de-aerated via bubbling with the N2 atmosphere (99.999%) before and during all measurements. The photovoltaic and electrical properties were investigated by a solar simulator (Abet Technologies model 10500, Inc., Milford, Connecticut). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed taken at 0.5 V by applying a sinusoidal signal with an amplitude of 10 mV under dark and light conditions; the frequency range of the signal was 0.1 Hz∼100 kHz. Fitting settings and simulate impedance data were performed by software Zview.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para:
The Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites were synthesized by a simple room temperature solid-state (RTSS) chemical reaction. For the synthesis of Gr|PANI, 10 mmol (1.30 g) PhNH2.HCl and 0.25 g graphite powder were weighed and grounded in an agate mortar. After grinding for 1 h in the surrounding environment, 25 mmol (6.75 g) FeCl3.6H2O powder was added to the reaction mixture. After additional grinding for 1 h in RT kept at RT without stirring for 12 h. The collected green-black powder was thoroughly washed by deionized water, filtered, and then dried under natural conditions for 12 h. Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposite have been synthesized using the same procedure. With the difference in the last step, 1 M sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution was added (pH = 11.0) to the resulting solid shacked for 30 min and then filtrated and dried in RT. It is verified that the value of pH has a significant role in the formation of Fe3O4 [25]. By adding NaOH to the reaction mixture, the color of the product was changed to dark brown; it has resulted in the immediate co-precipitation of ferric and ferrous ions according to Eq. (1) which represents the formation of Fe3O4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of an inexpensive FTO│TiO2│Gr|Fe3O4@PANI│Al solar cell
ce:section.ce:para: A thin film of TiO2 was deposited on the conductive side of fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO: as an anode) glass using a modified doctor-blade method [28]. A concentrated solution was prepared by dissolving the nanocomposite in NMP, and a very thin layer of the nanocomposite was deposited onto the TiO2 film by a modified doctor-blade method. Finally, Al (as the cathode) was put on the top of the resulting nanocomposite film to form a sandwich-type structure. The total area of each solar cell was 1 cm2, and the light intensity was 60 mW.cm−2 supplied by 200 W of Xenon lamp. Fig. 2 shows a schematic of the FTO│TiO2│Gr|Fe3O4@PANI│Al solar cell evaluated in this study.

The FT-IR spectra of the Gr|PANI and, Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 3 . For Gr|PANI, the bands at 1470 and 1554 cm–1 correspond to the aromatic C=C stretching vibration of benzenoid rings (N-B-N) and the C=C and C=N stretching vibration of the quinoid rings (N=Q=N), respectively.

The peaks that appeared at 1299 and 1240 cm–1 correspond to the C–N stretching vibration of secondary aromatic amines and -C=N●+- (or -C–N●+-) stretching vibration in polaron on the lattice, respectively. The peaks at 1131 correspond to the –NH+= group due to the high level of electron delocalization in B=NH+–B and/or Q–NH+–B moieties (bipolaron) and the hydrogen bonding between –N= and –NH+–. The bands corresponding to out-of-plane bending vibration C-H appear 800 cm−1 [26,29-31].

The Fe–O stretching band of magnetite is located at 605 cm−1 in the spectrum of Gr|Fe3O4@PANI, indicating Fe3O4 in the nanocomposite. It should be noted that in Gr|Fe3O4@PANI, PANI bands shift to higher wavenumbers. It can be corresponding to the interaction between Fe3O4 and PANI, which is ascribed to the interaction (P–N junction) between Fe3O4 and PANI [32–34]. To ensure the successful synthesis of Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles, we compared the FT-IR spectra of both Fe3O4 and Fe2O3. As in our previous work [27], Fe3O4 showed only one peak in the 605 cm−1 range. While Fe2O3 (γ-Fe2O3 and α-Fe2O3) shows two or three peaks between 500 and 700 cm−1 are related to its structure and size, and they are different from Fe3O4 [35–38].

The morphologies and structures of the Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites were investigated by SEM and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . Based on Fig. 4a, PANI is in the form of a mass structure on the surface of graphene, while in Fig. 4b, suggesting that the Fe3O4@PANI with a core (Fe3O4)-shell (PANI) structure with size ∼19 nm has been grown between the graphene sheets [27,39].
The TEM results for microstructure of the Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites were shown in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5a and b reveal that PANI and Fe3O4@PANI are coated on graphene sheets, respectively.

The UV-visible spectra of the graphite, Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites dispersed in NMP are shown in Fig. 6 . The spectra of both nanocomposites showing pronounced shoulder peaks at 285 nm are assigned to the π – π* transition of C=C bands in graphene [40]. The peak at 284 nm is assigned to the π – π* transition of C=C bands in graphite-neat, as is observed in Fig. 6. The peaks at 340 and 636 nm in curve were assigned to the π–π* transition within the benzenoid ring and n–π* electron transition and/or the exciton of benzenoid to quinoid rings of polyaniline in the structure of Gr|PANI, respectively [41–43]. In the curve of Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposite, these peaks are blue-shifted to 310 and 607 nm, respectively [44]. It indicates the NaOH effect on PANI-salt for converting it to PANI-base. The UV–Vis results are consistent with FT-IR.

The UV-visible spectra of the Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites dispersed in FA are shown in Fig. 7 a. The UV–Vis spectra of Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites show a shoulder peak at 252 and 251 nm, which corresponds to π – π* transition of the C=C in graphene [40]. The absorption peak at 300 nm should be related to π→π* electron transition of the quinoid and/or benzenoid rings of polyaniline in Gr|PANI. The absorption peaks in the range of 350-430 attributed to the polaron→π* transition and/or a redshift of π→π* electron transition of the benzenoid rings in the emeraldine salt (ES). Absorption peaks in the range of 700-900 nm should be related to localized π → polaron transition [26].

To determine the optical band gap (
E g
O p t
) of the Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites, a plot of (αhυ)1/
n vs. hυ [45], is shown in Fig. 6 (b and c) and 7 (b and c), respectively. The n factor depends on the nature of the electron transition and is equal to 2 and ½ for the indirect or direct transition band gaps [46–48]. Both curves (αhυ)1/2 vs. hυ and (αhυ)2 vs. hυ were plotted. The plot of (αhυ)1/2 vs. hυ had a better fit, as only the drawing of this curve is shown in Fig. 6 (b and c) and 7 (b and c). For both nanocomposites in NMP solvent, two band gaps were obtained (Fig. 6b and c): 1) The
E g
O p t
has obtained at 2.90 and 2.70 eV for Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites, respectively, which is attributed to π→π* transfers. 2) The
E g
O p t
has obtained at 1.51 and 1.52 eV for Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites, which is attributed to n→π* transfers. In formic acid solvent since this solvent acts as a dopant (protic solvent) and causes the formation of polarons and bipolarons due to the complete doping process [49], only one
E g
O p t
was observed which is attributed to transfers polaron→π*. As can be seen in Fig. 7b and c, the
E g
O p t
has obtained at 2.06 and 1.80 eV for Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites, respectively. Usually, direct transition and bandgap were observed for PANI derivatives and their composites [45,46]. However, indirect transition and bandgap have been found for Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites. The indirect nature of the
E g
O p t
in Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites can be due to the strong spin-orbit coupling by Fe3O4 and/or Gr in these nanocomposites. This causes intense absorption of light through phonons generated from Fe3O4; then, the excited electrons are relaxed into the conductive band (CB and/or LUMO) as triplet spin state, where they are protected from recombination [50].

As shown in Fig. 8 for Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites, the peak at 2ϴ value of 26.55° corresponds to the (002) plane of the graphene [51]. The XRD patterns of the Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposite provide information on the change of the crystal structure of Gr|PANI due to the presence of Fe3O4 in the structure Gr|Fe3O4@PANI. For Gr|Fe3O4@PANI, the peaks at 30.84o, 35.58o, 37.02o, 43.15o, 56.97o, and 62.27o are assigned to the (220), (311), (222), (400), (422), (511), and (440) planes of Fe3O4. Except for graphene and Fe3O4 peaks, no diffraction peak was observed for PANI that indicates the amorphous nature of PANI chains with broad and low intensity in the nanocomposite [52,53].

To examine the structure of graphene and graphite in detail, we compared our X-ray pattern with the literature. According to the literature [51,54], the crystal plane of GO appears with a spacing of 8.33 A˚. The spacing is 3.36 A˚ for graphite and graphene is 3.70A˚. For our nanocomposite, this spacing is equal to 3.45 A˚. This means that the polymer chains are somewhat trapped between the graphite sheets. In other literature reported with Mu et al. [44] which is similar to our work, the exfoliation of Graphite into mono or few-layered sheets due to the intercalation polymerization of aniline and the formation of Fe3O4 nanoparticles shows reduction of graphite/polymer peak intensity (compared to pure graphite powder).

In fact, one of the important chemical methods of graphene preparation is by separating the graphite layers through intercalation with various materials followed by exfoliation. This approach is also known as the ‘Scotch tape’ or peel-off method that is working on micromechanical exfoliation from patterned graphite in obtaining single-layer graphene sheets [55].
Furthermore, there are other similar examples from intercalation polymerization of aniline and the formation of Fe3O4 nanoparticles in preparation of PANI/Fe3O4 nanocomposites in literature [56–58].

Fig. 9 shows EDX spectra for the elemental analysis of Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI. The EDX pattern of Gr|Fe3O4@PANI is significantly confirmed that nanocomposite consists of the high content of Fe. This is compatible with TGA (next section).

The thermal stability of Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites were studied by TGA in The N2 atmosphere (Fig. 10 a and b). Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites exhibit a slight weight loss below 200°C owing to the due to the evaporation of physically adsorbed water. In the temperature range of 250–500°C, about 60 and 22% weight loss were observed for Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites, respectively, which attributed to principal thermal decomposition for the PANI and chemically (OH groups on the surface of the Fe3O4 NPs) adsorbed water. The degradation of PANI chains referred to the substituted aromatic fragments, formation of small aromatic fragments, the extended aromatic fragments, and the thermal decomposition for the gases and functional groups, such as CO, OH, and NH2 on the polymer blend backbone [59]. In the temperature range of 500–700°C, about 20 and 12% weight loss were observed for Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites, respectively, due to the decomposition of graphene sheets [60].
As a result, Gr|Fe3O4@PANI exhibits a mass loss of 36% between 25 and 900°C, while a mass loss of 98% for Gr|PANI. Residue about 64% from Gr|Fe3O4@PANI corresponds to Fe3O4.

Fig. 9a and b show the DSC results of Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI recorded from in nitrogen atmosphere. According to DSC analysis, both curves show two endothermic peaks. The endothermic peaks about 348 and 458°C correspond to decomposition or the thermal degradation associated with the polyaniline matrix in Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI, respectively [56,61,62]. Indeed, it involves PANI degradation by the breakdown of covalence bonds is led to the fragmentation of polymer chains into small and volatile molecules that are driven off from the sample.
Furthermore, the endothermic peak at 458 and/or 649 °C may correspond to the transformation of Fe3O4 into γ-Fe2O3 (and other ɛ-Fe2O3 and α-Fe2O3 phases) in the structure of Gr|Fe3O4@PANI [63].

After that, an endothermic DSC peak is observed at 617.59 and 649.55 °C for Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites, respectively, which is accompanied by a weight loss at the range of 550–800 °C. It is due to the decomposition of the graphene sheets [64].

The magnetic properties of Fe3O4 and the Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposite were obtained with a VSM. The hysteresis M–H curves are shown in Fig. 11. Saturation magnetizations are 58.20 and 29.84 emu g−1 for Fe3O4 and the Gr|Fe3O4@PANI, respectively. The decrease in saturation magnetization for the Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposite is due to the coating by the Gr and PANI.

Cyclic voltammetry studies of Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites have been recorded at a scan rate of 50 mV/s within potential range -0.2–1.0 V/Ag/AgCl (Fig. 12 ). In Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposite, the CV results show one oxidation peak at 0.62 V related to the oxidation of emeraldine to pernigraniline, as well as two reduction peaks at 0.33 and 0.14 V associated with the reduction of pernigreaniline to emeraldine and emeraldine to lecomeraldine, respectively, [65,66] revealing the formation of PANI in the graphene nanocomposite. In Gr|PANI nanocomposite, the first oxidation peak at 0.58 V, is related to the oxidation of emeraldine to pernigraniline form. The reduction peak (0.29 V) for Gr|PANI nanocomposite is due to the reduction of pernigraniline to emeraldine. The significant enhancement of the current between the electroactive species demonstrates a superior electrochemical activity of the Gr|Fe3O4@PANI. Therefore, the Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposite possesses a comparatively appreciable electrochemical response compared with Gr|PANI [67,68].

So, in the next step, we calculated the energy levels of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), and the band gap energies (

Besides experimental uncertainties, there is a slight difference between the

due to two the following reasons: 1) The oxidation process progressed through the separation units of holes and electrons and the redox process advanced through separated transporting units of electrons and holes and not in conjugated units. 2) The CV and UV–Vis spectra were recorded in solid-state and solution, respectively. In solution, some of the PANI chains take off from the graphene and/or Fe3O4 surface, and then, they are separated from each other [71,72]. In fact, the PANI chains have the same chemical structure, but they are different in their organization in solution and solid-state. Electrochemical data for the Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposite is shown in Table 1 .

Fig. 14 (a and b) shows the J–V characteristics of FTO│TiO2│Gr|PANI│Al and FTO│TiO2│Gr|Fe3O4@PANI│Al cell under illumination. The J-V characteristics as a function of the incident light intensity were used to obtain the short-circuit current density (Jsc), open-circuit voltage (Voc), maximum current density point (Jmax), and maximum voltage point (Vmax). The power conversion efficiency (η) of the solar cells was calculated from the following Eq. (5) [73]:

Fig. 15 shows a general schematic of the energy band diagram of FTO│TiO2│Gr|PANI│Al and FTO│TiO2│Gr|Fe3O4@PANI│Al cells.

As shown in Table 2, due to the presence of an efficient electron diffusion channel and the easy charge transfer between polyaniline and graphene, the photovoltaic activity of prepared nanocomposite and its solar cell energy conversion efficiency increased compared to polyaniline based solar cell. The large surface area of graphene and the presence of strong π-π interactions between the two components (the Gr is regarded as the electron acceptor, whereas PANI functions as an electron donor) [83], allows for a continuous pathway and multiple acceptor sites for effective electron transfer from PANI to Gr (Fig. 16 ). On the other hand, as mentioned in our previous works, the presence of Fe3O4 increases the triplet spin state population by spin-orbit coupling [26,27]. In other words, the efficient lifetime of exciton would be improved by increasing the efficiency of the intersystem crossing process in the solar cell. As shown in Table 3, the advantages of our solar cell in this work are simple structure, easy, inexpensive and green to fabricate, as well as a better efficiency.

Another significant advantage is the greater stability of our cells than other polymer cells. PANI is known as an excellent radical scavenging [84,85]. The basis for this favorable effect incorporates the theory that PANI can effectively neutralize free radicals, by donating electrons. In this route, the oxidation state of PANI was changed from half oxidized form of PANI (emeraldine) to a fully oxidized form of PANI (pernigraniline) as PANI-ES + HO-O● → PANI-PS + H2O [85,86].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impedance analysis (EIS) of the Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposite based solar cell
ce:section.ce:para:
Impedance (EIS) allows the analysis of surface changes caused by bio-cognition events on electrode surfaces through the capacitor and the surface electron transfer resistance of semi-conductive or conductive electrodes. The equivalent circuit used to fit the obtained impedance data as shown in the inset of Fig. 17 and Table 4 , where Rs, Rct, CPE, and W represent the ohmic serial resistance, charge transfer resistance, constant phase element, and Warburg impedance element, respectively. The Nyquist plots show a straight sloping line at low frequencies pronounced semicircle at the high frequencies in both the irradiated and the dark cases [87–90]. The diameters of the semicircles and the straight sloping line are related to the charge transfer resistance at the electrode surface, and the diffusion process of reactive species, respectively. As observed, the value of Rct for the Gr|Fe3O4@PANI electrode increases from 818 Ω•cm−2 in the visible light illumination to 858 Ω•cm−2 under dark, while the value of CPE decreases from 221 × 10−6 F•cm−2 in the visible light illumination to 196 × 10−6 F•cm−2 under dark manifesting as a faster charge transfer under visible light illumination due to the N-doping of Gr-PANI and Fe3O4 loading. Next, the charge carrier lifetime and/or the time constant is estimated by using Eq. (7) or its effective rate constant (keff) [87–90].
(7)
τ
D a r k .
L i g h t
=
1
2 π f
=
1 ω
=
1
k
e f f
Where f is the frequency [Hz] of the corresponding Nyquist maximum and ω is the angular frequency [1/s]. The large semicircular peak in the Cole-Cole plot corresponds to a frequency that mutually affects the carrier's lifetime. We have shown the time constant for the dark and light as τdark and τlight, respectively. For the Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposite, under dark and light, the effective carrier lifetime is determined to be 129.40 and 112.87 ms, respectively. According to the results, τdark is larger than τlight. It is necessary to consider what conditions these values refer to. The longer electron lifetime of Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposite shows that photogenerated carriers have more time to move forward. Finally, without suffering recombination, be collected by the cathode Al [87–90]. However, due to less resistance (Rs and Rct) and larger CPE, it seems that the sorter electron lifetime of Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposite in light shows that photogenerated carriers have fast moved to TiO2, and then to FTO as demonstrated in Fig. 15. In fact, the sorter electron lifetime may not necessarily be related to the faster recombination. In other words, according to the spin-flip mechanism we proposed earlier, the interaction between Fe3O4 and PANI makes to separate PP easily (coulombically bound polaron pairs) at the Gr|Fe3O4@PANI interfaces by conversion of PP photogenerated from singlet spin state to triplet spin state [26], as shown in Fig. 16. However, more studies are needed in this area that is ongoing in our laboratory.

In summary, Gr|PANI and Gr|Fe3O4@PANI nanocomposites were synthesized from graphite via a simple and green solid-state reaction method. A device with cell structure FTO│TiO2│Gr|Fe3O4@PANI│Al was fabricated and its application was tested. The photovoltaic cell characteristics, that is, Jsc, Voc, and η, were found to be 4.14 mA/cm2, 0.71 V, and 1.81, respectively. The combination of graphene with polyaniline in the resulted solar cell leads to unique properties such as low cost, high environmental stability, good conductivity, easy and green preparation, and high efficiency. An increase of Gr leads to an extension of the exciton's dissociation area and faster electron transfer through the graphene, and an increase of Fe3O4 leads to an increased triplet state of excitons via the spin-orbit coupling. Furthermore, an unusual indirect transition and bandgap have been found for nanocomposites arising from a strong spin-orbit coupling created by Fe3O4 and/or Gr in nanocomposites. This topic introduces nanocomposites as a desirable compound for organic solar cells.
